metadata.title: On the convergence of mSGD and AdaGrad for stochastic optimization

figures.description: SGD, mSGD, and AdaGrad update rules mathematical formulas

sections.title: Contributions:
content: We prove that the iterates of mSGD are asymptotically convergent to a connected set of stationary points for possibly non-convex loss function almost surely.
We quantify the convergence rate of mSGD for the loss functions. Through this convergence rate we can get a theoretical explanation of why mSGD can be seen as an acceleration of SGD.
We prove the iterates of AdaGrad are asymptotically convergent to a connected set of stationary points almost surely for possible non-convex loss functions.

sections.title: Main assumptions:
content: Assumption 1Noise sequence {ξn} are mutually independent and independent of θ1 and v0, such that g(x) = Eξn(g(x, ξn)) for any x ∈ ℝN.

Assumption 2 (Loss function assumption)Loss function g(θ) satisfies the following conditions: 1) g(θ) is non-negative and continuously differentiable. 2) The set of stationary points is not empty. 3) ∇g(θ) satisfies Lipschitz condition. 4) There is a scalar M > 0 for any θ and positive integer n.
Assumption 3Momentum coefficient α ∈ (0,1) and the sequence of step size εn is positive, monotonically decreasing to zero.
Assumption 4 (Loss function assumption)Loss function g(θ) satisfies the following conditions: 1) g(θ) is non-negative and continuously differentiable. 2) For any i ∈ {1,2,...,n}, it holds that...

Assumption 5Loss function g(θ) in equation 5 satisfies the following conditions: 1) g(θ) is non-negative and continuously differentiable. 2) The gradient ∇g(θ) satisfies the Lipschitz condition. 3) There are two constants M' > 0 and a > 0.

sections.title: Main results:
content: Theorem 1Consider the mSGD in equation 3. If Assumptions 1-3 hold, then for ∀θ1 ∈ ℝN and ∀v0 ∈ ℝN, there exists a connected set J* ⊆ J such that the iterate θn is convergent to the set J* almost surely.
Theorem 2Consider the mSGD in equation 3 with the noise following a uniform sampling distribution. If Assumptions 1-4 hold, for ∀v0 ∈ ℝN and ∀θ1 ∈ ℝN, it holds that:

Theorem 3Consider the AdaGrad in equation 5. If Assumptions 1 and 5 hold, then for ∀θ1 ∈ ℝN and S0 = 0, there exists a connected component of the set J* ⊆ J, such that the estimate θn is convergent to the set J* almost surely.

figures.description: Theorem 2 convergence rate formula and equation 10

sections.title: Proof outline for Theorems 1-2:
content: The proof is in light of the Lyapunov method. We aim to prove that g(θn) is convergent a.s., and then to prove ∇g(θn) → 0 a.s. With these two results, we are able to get θn → J* a.s.
Step 1:We prove mSGD is a stable algorithm, i.e., E(g(θn)) < +∞, ∀n, in Lemma 1. The idea is to prove that a weighted sum of the loss function value, i.e.,
is bounded through a recursion formula (a rough form).
Step 2:From Lemma 1 and Lemma 8, we are able to prove that ΣA(n)||vn||² < +∞, we are able to prove that Σ||vn||² and Σ||∇g(θn)||² are convergent a.s. respectively, as stated in Lemma 7 and Lemma 8.
Step 3:We divide g(θn) into three terms:
Step 4:By Lemma 9 and the convergence of g(θn) in martingale-difference sum (Lemma 5), we prove that ΣCn(∇g(θn, ξn) - ∇g(θn)) is convergent a.s. Then we prove g(θn) is convergent a.s.

Step 5:After the proof of the convergence of mSGD, we analyze the iterates of Fn. Then under a new assumption lim inf ||∇g(θ)||²/ (g(θ) - gi) ≥ s > 0, we are able to obtain the convergent rate of mSGD.
